title: Birth, Death, and Replication at Surfaces: Universal Laws of Autocatalytic Dynamics
abstract: Autocatalytic processes underlie diverse systems in which replication is triggered at interfaces, including heterogeneous catalysis on solid substrates, enzyme activity at membranes, viral infections, biofilm growth, and spatially structured ecosystems. In a typical scenario, particles move in a bulk medium and interact with surface regions, where they may either disappear or reproduce through branching, splitting or fission. Here, we develop a general theoretical framework to understand such surface-mediated autocatalytic processes. We show that the interplay between loss and replication at surfaces gives rise to rich population dynamics. For this purpose, we derive a renewal-type nonlinear integral equation for the generating function of the population size, providing access to its full probability distribution and statistical moments. We further establish an equivalent description in terms of a Fokker-Planck equation with nonlinear Robin-type boundary conditions that encode surface reactions. Our results identify distinct dynamical regimes and universal scaling laws, and provide a unified framework to predict when surface activity promotes extinction or explosive growth. These findings offer quantitative insight into catalytic efficiency, metabolic regulation, and population persistence in spatially heterogeneous environments.
summary: This paper provides a general theory for autocatalytic processes occurring at interfaces, such as enzyme activity or heterogeneous catalysis. By using a generating function approach, the author derives a backward Fokker-Planck equation with nonlinear boundary conditions. The study identifies three distinct regimes—subcritical, critical, and supercritical—based on the competition between surface absorption and branching. These results offer a unified way to predict whether surface activity leads to population extinction or exponential growth.
main_problem: Developing a unified theoretical framework to understand surface-mediated autocatalytic processes where replication and loss are triggered at interfaces.
main_result: The derivation of a nonlinear integral equation and an equivalent backward Fokker-Planck equation with nonlinear Robin-type boundary conditions, identifying three universal asymptotic regimes: subcritical, critical, and supercritical.
method: A probabilistic approach using generating functions, renewal-type integral equations, and spectral analysis of the diffusion operator.
model_or_system: A diffusive population of particles in a bulk medium interacting with a partitioned boundary consisting of absorbing, catalytic (branching), and inert regions.
key_observables: Generating function G_s(t|x_0), probability distribution of population size Q_k(t|x_0), mean population size N_1(t|x_0), and survival probability.
important_parameters: Surface reactivities (kappa_m), diffusion coefficient (D), and the principal eigenvalue (lambda_0) which determines the dynamical regime.
main_assumptions: Particles undergo ordinary diffusion with a constant diffusivity; the drift vector and diffusion matrix are time-independent and smooth.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of a partitioned surface with different reaction zones; Figure 2 illustrates population dynamics (survival probability, distribution, and moments) in a hollow cylinder across subcritical, critical, and supercritical regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of surface-mediated autocatalysis, develops a mathematical framework using generating functions and renewal equations, transforms these into a PDE description with nonlinear boundary conditions, performs spectral analysis to identify asymptotic regimes, and validates the theory with numerical simulations in a cylindrical geometry.
why_interesting: The framework for nonlinear boundary conditions and the transition between extinction and explosive growth is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and non-equilibrium dynamics where boundaries drive population-like changes in quantum states or quasiparticle numbers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A schematic view of autocatalytic reactions on a sur- face partitioned into three subsets (here, M = 2): an absorb- ing region Γ0 (in red) that destroys particles via A+Γ0 →Γ0; an inert region Γ1 (in gray) that reflects particles back into the bulk; and a catalytic region Γ2 (in green) that replicates particles via a binary splitting A + Γ2 →2A + Γ2. Each of three subsets can be composed of multiple pieces. In the shown random realization, a single particle started from x0 diffuses towards the surface and then branches into two copies on Γ2; one of them reaches an absorbing region Γ0 and dis- appears, whereas the other produces two more offsprings on another piece of the catalytic...

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the population size N(t) for a diffusion-reaction system with diffusivity D = 1 in a hol- low cylinder of radii R = 0.2 and L = 1, with the outer perfectly absorbing surface (κ0 = ∞) and the inner au- tocatalytic surface with reactivity κ2. The starting point x0 is on the inner surface. Three regimes are shown for κ2 = 0.9 κc 2 (subcritical, dash-dotted line), κ2 = κc 2 (criti- cal, dashed line), and κ2 = 1.1 κc 2 (supercritical, solid line), with κc 2 = D/(R ln(L/R)) ≈3.11.
